nativeRange.habitat: Like other Bucephalandra, the parent stock is endemic to the island of Borneo, where wild populations grow as rheophytes on rocks and submerged wood along the banks and beds of fast-flowing rainforest streams; aquarium-trade 'Black Pearl' plants are typically nursery-propagated from this lineage rather than wild-collected

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape to attach the rhizome to using super glue gel, sewing thread, or fishing line
moderate flow across the rhizome to mimic rheophyte stream conditions
shaded or moderately lit areas; commonly placed in the foreground or midground of nano and mid-size tanks
stable, established tank — generally advised not to introduce into newly cycled systems

goodWith: shrimp (Neocaridina and Caridina) which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern and mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: Bucephalandra Black Pearl is a small, slow-growing cultivar prized for its dark, almost black-green oblong leaves and fine iridescent speckled sheen. Care mirrors the broader genus: the rhizome is attached to rock or wood, kept in low to medium light, and must never be buried in substrate.
careNotes: Bucephalandra Black Pearl is a trade-named cultivar distinguished by small, oblong leaves that range from dark green to nearly black, each with a raised central vein and a fine iridescent white-speckled sheen that becomes visible under good light. Husbandry is identical to the broader genus: the creeping rhizome (the thick horizontal stem from which leaves and roots emerge) is attached to lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape using super glue gel, sewing thread, or fishing line, then left exposed to the water column. Burying the rhizome is generally reported to cause rot. Water parameters generally accepted for the cultivar fall in the pH 6.0–7.5 range at roughly 22–28 °C; a general hardness (gH) of around 3–15 °dGH is well tolerated. Mature clumps commonly stay within the 5–10 cm height range, placing it on the smaller end of the genus. Like other Bucephalandra, 'Black Pearl' is prone to a temporary melt of pre-existing leaves when first transitioned from emersed nursery growth into a submerged aquarium, or after sudden swings in temperature, pH, or TDS (total dissolved solids); as long as the rhizome stays firm and pale green or white inside, new submerged-form leaves are generally reported to emerge over the following weeks. The plant is best introduced into stable, established tanks rather than newly set-up systems, and care is widely considered similar in difficulty to Anubias.
breedingNotes: The most practical method of propagation is rhizome division. Using sharp, clean scissors, cut the rhizome at a natural bend or constriction so that each resulting piece retains several leaves and a set of visible roots, then reattach the new section to a separate piece of hardscape. Sexual reproduction via the genus' characteristic spathe and spadix (the flower-like structure typical of the Araceae family) is occasionally observed in emersed and paludarium setups but is not a practical propagation method for most hobbyists.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source. Describes 'Black Pearl' as a rare, farm-raised nano Bucephalandra cultivar with dark green-to-almost-red small rounded leaves, low-light tolerance, slow growth via rhizome, and care similar to Anubias (rhizome attached to hardscape, never buried). Page does not provide explicit temperature/pH/GH ranges.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked cultivar identity (dark oblong leaves with raised central vein and iridescent white-speckled appearance), pH 6.0-7.5, low-to-medium lighting, CO2 not necessary but recommended, slow growth, propagation by rhizome division with sharp scissors, epiphytic mounting on driftwood or rock, easy care, and warning that burying the rhizome causes rot/melt.

additional.name: AquaMate genus entry (Bucephalandra, fw-plant-016)
additional.notes: Husbandry shared with the genus per playbook note. Genus-level parameter ranges (temperature 22-28 °C, pH 6.0-7.5, gH 3-15 °dGH, low-light, optional CO2, slow growth, rhizome attached to hardscape) are applied here because the two cultivar-specific sources confirm 'same care as Anubias / genus' and do not contradict any genus values.
